title: Chicken Stock
intro: Good chicken stock is the backbone of soups, sauces, and braises. This method extracts collagen quickly under pressure, then finishes with a short aromatic simmer to layer in vegetable and herb flavor. When properly made and chilled, the stock sets into a soft gel that jiggles in the container, a sign that gelatin and body have been fully extracted.
ingredients: 1 whole chicken carcass (breast and thigh meat removed)
1 carrot, peeled and cut into large pieces
1 celery stalk, cut into large pieces
1 medium onion, peeled and cut in half
steps: Add the drumsticks to a pressure cooker.
Cut the chicken into parts and reserve the breasts and thighs for another use. Add the wings, drumsticks, carcass, and any skin or bones from the thighs and breasts to the pressure cooker.
Add 2½ quarts cold water and the apple cider vinegar.
Bring the cooker to 10 pounds of pressure and cook for 50 minutes.
Turn off the heat and allow the pressure cooker to cool naturally without releasing the steam.
When the cooker is cool enough to open but still hot, pour the contents into a large stock pot.
Add the carrot, celery, onion, garlic, bay leaf, parsley, thyme, peppercorns, and the 2 quarts of hot water.
Bring to a gentle simmer, cover, and cook for 1 hour.
Strain out and discard all solids.
Place the pot of strained stock in a sink or basin filled with ice water and stir occasionally to release heat and cool the stock quickly.
Transfer the cooled stock to the refrigerator and chill overnight.
Lift off and discard the hardened fat layer the next day.
The finished stock should set into a soft gel when chilled. Store in the refrigerator for several days or freeze for longer storage.
